Concurrency (computer science)

Concurrency in computer science refers to the ability of different parts or units of a program, algorithm, or problem to be executed out-of-order or in partial order, without affecting the outcome. This allows for parallel execution of concurrent units, which can significantly improve overall speed in multi-processor and multi-core systems.

Contents

  • 1 History
  • 2 Models of concurrency
  • 3 Issues
  • 4 See also
  • 5 References

History

The concept of concurrency has evolved significantly since the early days of computing. Edsger Dijkstra's 1965 paper on the dining philosophers problem and the introduction of semaphores laid foundational concepts for concurrent programming.

In 1973, Carl Hewitt introduced the actor model, which treats "actors" as the universal primitives of concurrent computation. Tony Hoare's 1978 paper on Communicating Sequential Processes (CSP) presented an alternative model based on message passing.

Models of concurrency

Several models exist for understanding and implementing concurrency:

  • Threads — lightweight units of execution within a process that share memory space
  • Actor model — independent entities that communicate through asynchronous message passing
  • CSP — processes communicate through channels using synchronous message passing
  • Software Transactional Memory — memory access is organized into atomic transactions
  • Coroutines — cooperative multitasking where routines voluntarily yield control

Issues

Concurrent programming introduces several challenges:

  • Race conditions — when the system's behavior depends on the sequence or timing of uncontrollable events
  • Deadlocks — when two or more processes are each waiting for the other to release a resource
  • Starvation — when a process is perpetually denied necessary resources
  • Priority inversion — when a low-priority task holds a resource needed by a high-priority task

See also

  • Parallel computing
  • Distributed computing
  • Mutex

References

  1. Dijkstra, E. W. (1965). "Solution of a problem in concurrent programming control". Communications of the ACM. 8 (9): 569.
  2. Hewitt, Carl (1973). "A Universal Modular ACTOR Formalism for Artificial Intelligence". IJCAI.
  3. Hoare, C. A. R. (1978). "Communicating sequential processes". Communications of the ACM. 21 (8): 666–677.
